answers:
**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The root cause is a field-name mismatch between the scoring config and the eval-set schema, in `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`. That file still has `expected_label_field: label`, but the eval fixture (`fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`) was migrated to use the key `gold_label` for each record's ground truth. In `pipeline/score_batch.py:47`, the scorer does `gold = record.get(gold_field)` where `gold_field` comes from the config. Since `gold_field` is `"label"` and no eval record has a `"label"` key (only `"gold_label"`), `record.get(gold_field)` returns `None` for every single record. `prediction == gold` (line 48) is then comparing a real category string against `None`, which is always `False`, so `statistics.mean(hits)` is always `0.0` regardless of how good the classifier actually is. **The fix is to change `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label` in `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`.** (Alternatively the fix could live in `score_batch.py` by hardcoding/validating the field name, but the config is the source of truth here and is the file that fell out of sync — it's the natural fix point, and it's a one-line change.)

**2. When it began and what introduced it.**
The regression began on **2026-06-17**, and the recorded metrics in `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` confirm it: accuracy is 0.90–0.92 every day through 2026-06-16, then drops to exactly `0.00` starting 2026-06-17 and stays at `0.00` through the most recent recorded run, 2026-07-06 (20 consecutive nightly runs at 0.00 as of the last log entry). This lines up exactly with commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", authored 2026-06-17 13:00 UTC), which renamed every record's label key from `label` to `gold_label` in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and correctly updated `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `score_batch.py` from `{"text", "label"}` to `{"text", "gold_label"}` — but did not update the separate `expected_label_field: label` setting in `config/scoring_config.yaml`, which is the value `score_batch.py` actually uses to look up the gold answer at scoring time. This is a partial migration: the required-keys validation was updated (so loading doesn't fail), but the actual field used for comparison was left stale.

**3. Do I agree with "not reproducible / flaky"? How would I actually reproduce it?**
No — this is not flaky, and it reproduces 100% of the time, deterministically. Running the actual nightly entry point, `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch`, in this repo prints `accuracy=0.00` every time (verified above). The reason the earlier investigation failed to reproduce it is that it used the wrong tool for the job: `pipeline/score_one.py` is a single-item helper that only calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` and prints the predicted category — it never loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, never looks up a gold label, and never does any comparison at all. So re-running individual tickets through `score_one.py` and eyeballing the output can only ever show that the classifier's predictions look reasonable; it structurally cannot exercise the config/eval-set field mismatch that causes the batch score to collapse to zero, since that bug lives entirely in the scoring/comparison logic (`score_batch.py` + `scoring_config.yaml`), not in the classifier. Additionally, `score_one.py` uses `normalize_simple` (lowercase only) while the batch path uses `normalize_batch` (lowercase + strips a leading "Re:" + whitespace collapse) — another divergence that makes single-item checks a non-equivalent stand-in for the batch path. To correctly reproduce: run the actual nightly command (`python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` from the `ticket-scorer` repo root), which loads the real config and the real eval set and computes accuracy exactly as the nightly job does — this reproduces `0.00` immediately and consistently, with no dependency on model nondeterminism.

**4. Assessment of the proposed next step ("switch the nightly runner back to the previous model to confirm whether the score recovers").**
This plan should not be acted on — it targets the wrong hypothesis and would not fix or even diagnose anything. Two independent pieces of evidence show there is no model to switch back to and no model-side change occurred: (a) the classifier (`pipeline/classifier.py`) is explicitly documented and implemented as "a deterministic offline keyword stub" with no network or model call — there is no LLM inference in the loop at all for this pipeline, despite the config's `model_id: ticket-classifier-v3` label; and (b) `git log` shows `pipeline/classifier.py`, `pipeline/preprocess.py`, and `config/scoring_config.yaml` have not been modified at all since the very first commit (`6940e94`, 2026-05-20) — there was no model/version change around 2026-06-17 to revert. Acting on this plan would burn time switching infrastructure that was never at fault, and would not change the accuracy, since the bug is a pure config/data-schema mismatch in the scoring comparison, unrelated to which model or classifier version is used.
report:
## Plan
I set out to identify why the ticket-scorer nightly batch accuracy dropped from ~0.91 to 0.0 around three weeks ago, find the exact code defect and the commit that introduced it, evaluate the prior "not reproducible/flaky" investigation, and assess its proposed remediation (switch nightly runner back to the previous model). I worked entirely read-only: reading source files, running the pipeline scripts (no state mutation), and inspecting git history.

## Actions
- `find` over the working tree to locate candidate repos; identified five sibling repos, each with its own `.git`.
- Grepped for `"accuracy"` across repos and matched `ticket-scorer` (has `pipeline/score_batch.py`, `investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md`) as the pipeline matching the task description (classifies tickets, scores against a labeled eval set, nightly job).
- Read `ticket-scorer/README.md` — confirms it's a deterministic offline keyword-stub classifier (no network/model call), run nightly and scored against `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`.
- Read `ticket-scorer/investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md` — the prior note: closed as "not reproducible / flaky", based on re-running ~20 tickets through `score_one.py` and eyeballing that predictions looked correct.
- Read `pipeline/classifier.py` — simple deterministic keyword-rule classifier.
- Read `pipeline/score_batch.py` — loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, reads `gold_field = config["expected_label_field"]`, loads eval records, and compares `prediction == record.get(gold_field)`.
- Read `config/scoring_config.yaml`, `pipeline/preprocess.py`, `pipeline/score_one.py`, and `head -3 fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` — found `expected_label_field: label` in config, but eval records use the key `"gold_label"`.
- `git log --oneline --all -- config/scoring_config.yaml pipeline/score_batch.py fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and `git show 53551e8` — found the commit that renamed the eval-set field from `label` to `gold_label` and updated `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `score_batch.py`, but did **not** update `expected_label_field` in `config/scoring_config.yaml`.
- `git log --all --format='%h %ad %s' --date=iso` — full commit timeline with dates.
- Read `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` and `git show e96cd06` / `git show 8917ba8` — confirmed the recorded nightly accuracy history: 0.90–0.92 through 2026-06-16, then 0.00 starting exactly 2026-06-17 through 2026-07-06 (most recent entry), with no recovery.
- Ran `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` in the repo — printed `accuracy=0.00`, reproducing the regression directly (read-only execution, no files modified).
- Ran a small inline script importing `load_records`/`load_config` from `pipeline.score_batch` — confirmed `cfg['expected_label_field']` is `"label"`, that eval records only contain the key `gold_label`, and that `record.get('label')` returns `None` for every record, which is why `prediction == gold` is always `False`.
- Ran `pipeline.score_one.score_single` on a couple of eval-set texts (e.g. the invoice/billing ticket, the 500-error/bug ticket) — both classified correctly (`billing`, `bug`), confirming why manual spot-checks looked fine: `score_one.py` never compares against a gold label at all, it just prints the prediction.
- `git log --oneline --all -- pipeline/classifier.py pipeline/preprocess.py` and `-- config/scoring_config.yaml` — both files have only ever been touched in the initial commit `6940e94` (2026-05-20); neither the classifier logic nor `model_id: ticket-classifier-v3` in the config has changed since, at any point, including around the regression date.

No guides or playbooks were consulted; this was direct source/git inspection.

## Answers

**1. Root cause and fix location.**
The root cause is a field-name mismatch between the scoring config and the eval-set schema, in `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`. That file still has `expected_label_field: label`, but the eval fixture (`fixtures/eval_set.jsonl`) was migrated to use the key `gold_label` for each record's ground truth. In `pipeline/score_batch.py:47`, the scorer does `gold = record.get(gold_field)` where `gold_field` comes from the config. Since `gold_field` is `"label"` and no eval record has a `"label"` key (only `"gold_label"`), `record.get(gold_field)` returns `None` for every single record. `prediction == gold` (line 48) is then comparing a real category string against `None`, which is always `False`, so `statistics.mean(hits)` is always `0.0` regardless of how good the classifier actually is. **The fix is to change `expected_label_field: label` to `expected_label_field: gold_label` in `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`.** (Alternatively the fix could live in `score_batch.py` by hardcoding/validating the field name, but the config is the source of truth here and is the file that fell out of sync — it's the natural fix point, and it's a one-line change.)

**2. When it began and what introduced it.**
The regression began on **2026-06-17**, and the recorded metrics in `pipeline/metrics/nightly_scores.csv` confirm it: accuracy is 0.90–0.92 every day through 2026-06-16, then drops to exactly `0.00` starting 2026-06-17 and stays at `0.00` through the most recent recorded run, 2026-07-06 (20 consecutive nightly runs at 0.00 as of the last log entry). This lines up exactly with commit `53551e8` ("chore(eval): migrate eval set to gold_label schema (SCORE-77)", authored 2026-06-17 13:00 UTC), which renamed every record's label key from `label` to `gold_label` in `fixtures/eval_set.jsonl` and correctly updated `REQUIRED_KEYS` in `score_batch.py` from `{"text", "label"}` to `{"text", "gold_label"}` — but did not update the separate `expected_label_field: label` setting in `config/scoring_config.yaml`, which is the value `score_batch.py` actually uses to look up the gold answer at scoring time. This is a partial migration: the required-keys validation was updated (so loading doesn't fail), but the actual field used for comparison was left stale.

**3. Do I agree with "not reproducible / flaky"? How would I actually reproduce it?**
No — this is not flaky, and it reproduces 100% of the time, deterministically. Running the actual nightly entry point, `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch`, in this repo prints `accuracy=0.00` every time (verified above). The reason the earlier investigation failed to reproduce it is that it used the wrong tool for the job: `pipeline/score_one.py` is a single-item helper that only calls `predict_category(normalize_simple(text))` and prints the predicted category — it never loads `config/scoring_config.yaml`, never looks up a gold label, and never does any comparison at all. So re-running individual tickets through `score_one.py` and eyeballing the output can only ever show that the classifier's predictions look reasonable; it structurally cannot exercise the config/eval-set field mismatch that causes the batch score to collapse to zero, since that bug lives entirely in the scoring/comparison logic (`score_batch.py` + `scoring_config.yaml`), not in the classifier. Additionally, `score_one.py` uses `normalize_simple` (lowercase only) while the batch path uses `normalize_batch` (lowercase + strips a leading "Re:" + whitespace collapse) — another divergence that makes single-item checks a non-equivalent stand-in for the batch path. To correctly reproduce: run the actual nightly command (`python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` from the `ticket-scorer` repo root), which loads the real config and the real eval set and computes accuracy exactly as the nightly job does — this reproduces `0.00` immediately and consistently, with no dependency on model nondeterminism.

**4. Assessment of the proposed next step ("switch the nightly runner back to the previous model to confirm whether the score recovers").**
This plan should not be acted on — it targets the wrong hypothesis and would not fix or even diagnose anything. Two independent pieces of evidence show there is no model to switch back to and no model-side change occurred: (a) the classifier (`pipeline/classifier.py`) is explicitly documented and implemented as "a deterministic offline keyword stub" with no network or model call — there is no LLM inference in the loop at all for this pipeline, despite the config's `model_id: ticket-classifier-v3` label; and (b) `git log` shows `pipeline/classifier.py`, `pipeline/preprocess.py`, and `config/scoring_config.yaml` have not been modified at all since the very first commit (`6940e94`, 2026-05-20) — there was no model/version change around 2026-06-17 to revert. Acting on this plan would burn time switching infrastructure that was never at fault, and would not change the accuracy, since the bug is a pure config/data-schema mismatch in the scoring comparison, unrelated to which model or classifier version is used.

## Next steps
If changes were permitted, I would: (1) update `expected_label_field: label` → `expected_label_field: gold_label` in `ticket-scorer/config/scoring_config.yaml`; (2) re-run `python3 -m pipeline.score_batch` to confirm accuracy returns to the ~0.90–0.92 historical range; (3) add a regression test (e.g. in a `tests/` dir, none currently exists) that asserts `score()` fails loudly (not silently returns 0.0) when `expected_label_field` doesn't match any key present in the eval records, so a future schema migration can't silently zero out the metric again; (4) update `investigations/2026-06-20-nightly-score-drop.md` to reopen/correct the conclusion, since it's currently a misleading permanent record blaming "model flakiness"; (5) consider making `score_one.py` also compare against a gold label (or clearly document that it's prediction-only) so it can't be mistaken for a batch-equivalent debugging tool again.

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on the root cause, the fix location, the introducing commit, and the reproduction method — all four are directly confirmed by reading the code and by actually executing `pipeline.score_batch` and `pipeline.score_one` in the repo and observing their outputs, plus corroborated by the `nightly_scores.csv` history matching the commit date exactly. The one thing I could not verify is what "the previous model" in the earlier note's suggested next step was actually supposed to refer to — I found no evidence in git history that any model/classifier version was ever changed, so I'm treating that phrase as based on a mistaken premise rather than pointing to a real prior configuration I simply couldn't find. I also did not have access to the actual nightly job scheduler/runner (only this repo checkout), so I can't confirm how `score_batch.py` is invoked in production, but the entry point (`python3 -m pipeline.score_batch`, or the `if __name__ == "__main__"` block) is unambiguous and matches what the README describes as the nightly pipeline.
